is the native language of the [Shan people](/wiki/Shan_people "Shan people") and is mostly spoken in [Shan State](/wiki/Shan_State "Shan State"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"). It is also spoken in pockets in other parts of Myanmar, in [Northern Thailand](/wiki/Northern_Thailand "Northern Thailand"), in [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan"), in [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), in [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), in [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") and decreasingly in [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") and [Meghalaya](/wiki/Meghalaya "Meghalaya"). Shan is a member of the [Kra–Dai language family](/wiki/Kra%E2%80%93Dai_languages "Kra–Dai languages") and is related to [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"). It has five tones, which do not correspond exactly to Thai tones, plus a sixth tone used for emphasis. The term Shan is also used for related Northwestern Tai languages, and it is called **Tai Yai** or **Tai Long** in other Tai languages. Standard Shan, which is also known as Tachileik Shan, is based on the dialect of the city of [Tachileik](/wiki/Tachileik "Tachileik").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

**Shan** is the native language of the [Shan people](/wiki/Shan_people "Shan people") and is mostly spoken in [Shan State](/wiki/Shan_State "Shan State"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"). It is also spoken in pockets in other parts of Myanmar, in [Northern Thailand](/wiki/Northern_Thailand "Northern Thailand"), in [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan"), in [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), in [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), in [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") and decreasingly in [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") and [Meghalaya](/wiki/Meghalaya "Meghalaya"). Shan is a member of the [Kra–Dai language family](/wiki/Kra%E2%80%93Dai_languages "Kra–Dai languages") and is related to [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"). It has five tones, which do not correspond exactly to Thai tones, plus a sixth tone used for emphasis. The term Shan is also used for related Northwestern Tai languages, and it is called **Tai Yai** or **Tai Long** in other Tai languages. Standard Shan, which is also known as Tachileik Shan, is based on the dialect of the city of [Tachileik](/wiki/Tachileik "Tachileik").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2019, Ethnologue estimated there were 3.3 million Shan speakers, including 3.2 million in Myanmar.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[1]](#cite_note-e25-1) The [Mahidol University](/wiki/Mahidol_University "Mahidol University") Institute for Language and Culture estimates there are gave the number of Shan speakers in Thailand as 95,000 in 2006.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Many Shan speak local dialects as well as the language of their trading partners.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Tai peoples](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") and [Shan people](/wiki/Shan_people "Shan people")

Historically, the dominance of Shan as a regional lingua franca made it the source of many loanwords in other regional languages, especially [Jingpo](/wiki/Jingpo_language "Jingpo language") and [Palaung](/wiki/Palaung_language "Palaung language").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

### Influence from Burmese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Influence from Burmese")]

By the same token, Shan has been significantly influenced by Burmese, mediated by centuries of historical and ongoing contact and exchange between Burmese and Shan speakers, especially between the Burmese royal court and Shan principalities.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) For instance, the lack of a /f/ sound in most Shan dialects is attributed to Burmese influence; this sound is present in the closely related [Khün](/wiki/Kh%C3%BCn_language "Khün language") and [Northern Thai languages](/wiki/Northern_Thai_language "Northern Thai language"). Shan vocabulary has been significantly enriched by Burmese contact, with Burmese loan words appearing throughout the Shan lexicon,[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) including loanwords borrowed from [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali") via Burmese. Burmese appears to have also influenced Shan grammar, with respect to the use of complex prepositions and certain word patterns that do not exist in closely related Tai languages.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

Due to Shan's status as a lingua franca in the region, it has served as an intermediary, passing loanwords from [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language") into other regional languages.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

### Influence from Thai

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Influence from Thai")]

Due to labour migration in recent decades, one million ethnic Shan now live in Thailand.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) As a result of ongoing language contact, [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") has increasingly become a competing source of loanwords into Shan, especially for scientific and political concepts.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) These Thai loanwords are often more difficult to detect, because of phonetic and structural similarities between Shan and Thai.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Some recent phonological developments, like the reversal of the historical /f/ > /[pʰ](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant")/ shift especially among younger Shan speakers, is attributed to contact with Thai.

## Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Names")]

The Shan language has a number of names in different [Tai languages](/wiki/Tai_languages "Tai languages") and [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language").

* In Shan, the spoken language is commonly called *kwam tai* (ၵႂၢမ်းတႆး, [[kwáːm.táj]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Shan_and_Tai_Lue "Help:IPA/Shan and Tai Lue"), lit. '[Tai](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") language'). The written language is called *lik tai* (လိၵ်ႈတႆး, [[lik táj]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Shan_and_Tai_Lue "Help:IPA/Shan and Tai Lue")).
* In [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"), it is called *hram: bhasa* (ရှမ်းဘာသာ, [[ʃáɰ̃ bàðà]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Burmese "Help:IPA/Burmese")), whence the English word "Shan". The term "Shan," which was formerly spelt *hsyam:* (သျှမ်း) in Burmese, is an [exonym](/wiki/Exonym "Exonym") believed to be a Burmese derivative of "Siam" (an old term for [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand")).
* In [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") and [Southern Thai](/wiki/Southern_Thai_language "Southern Thai language"), it is called *phasa thai yai* (ภาษาไทใหญ่, [[pʰāː.sǎː.tʰāj.jàj]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai"), lit. 'big/great [Tai](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") language') or more informally or even vulgarly by some *phasa ngiao* (ภาษาเงี้ยว, [[pʰāː.sǎː.ŋía̯w]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai"), an outdated term that now sounds like the word for "snake").
* In [Northern Thai](/wiki/Northern_Thai_language "Northern Thai language"), it is called *kam tai* (กำไต, [[kām.tāj]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Northern_Thai "Help:IPA/Northern Thai"), literally "[Tai](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") language") or more informally or even vulgarly by some *kam ngiao* (กำเงี้ยว, [[kām.ŋía̯w]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Northern_Thai "Help:IPA/Northern Thai")), lit. 'Shan language').
* In [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"), it is called *phasa tai yai* (ພາສາໄທໃຫຍ່, [[pʰáː.sǎː.tʰáj.ɲāj]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Lao "Help:IPA/Lao"), lit. 'big/great [Tai](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") language') or more informally or even vulgarly by some *phasa ngiao* (ພາສາງ້ຽວ, [[pʰáː.sǎː.ŋîa̯w]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Lao "Help:IPA/Lao")).
* In [Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_Lue_language "Tai Lue language"), it is called *kam ngio* (ᦅᧄᦇᦲᧁᧉ, [[kâm.ŋìw]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Shan_and_Tai_Lue "Help:IPA/Shan and Tai Lue")).

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Dialects")]

The Shan dialects spoken in [Shan State](/wiki/Shan_State "Shan State") can be divided into three groups, roughly coinciding with geographical and modern administrative boundaries, namely the northern, southern, and eastern dialects. Dialects differ to a certain extent in vocabulary and pronunciation, but are generally mutually intelligible.

While the southern dialect has borrowed more [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language") words, eastern Shan is somewhat closer to [Northern Thai language](/wiki/Northern_Thai_language "Northern Thai language") and [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language") in vocabulary and pronunciation, and the northern so-called "[Chinese Shan](/wiki/Tai_Nuea_language "Tai Nuea language")" is much influenced by the Yunnan-Chinese dialect.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

A number of words differ in initial consonants. In the north, initial /k/, /kʰ/ and /m/, when combined with certain vowels and final consonants, are pronounced /tʃ/ (written *ky*), /tʃʰ/ (written *khy*) and /mj/ (written *my*). In Chinese Shan, initial /n/ becomes /l/. In southwestern regions /m/ is often pronounced as /w/. Initial /f/ only appears in the east, while in the other two dialects it merges with /pʰ/.

[J. Marvin Brown](/wiki/J._Marvin_Brown "J. Marvin Brown") divides the three dialects of Shan State as follows:[[5]](#cite_note-5)

1. Northern — [Lashio](/wiki/Lashio "Lashio"), Burma; contains more Chinese influences
2. Southern — [Taunggyi](/wiki/Taunggyi "Taunggyi"), Burma (capital of [Shan State](/wiki/Shan_State "Shan State")); contains more Burmese influences
3. Eastern — [Kengtung](/wiki/Kengtung "Kengtung"), Burma (in the [Golden Triangle](/wiki/Golden_Triangle_(Southeast_Asia) "Golden Triangle (Southeast Asia)")); closer to Northern Thai and Lao

Prominent divergent dialects are considered separate languages, such as [Khün](/wiki/Kh%C3%BCn_language "Khün language") (called Kon Shan by the Burmese), which is spoken in [Kengtung](/wiki/Kengtung_Township "Kengtung Township") valley. Chinese Shan is also called Tai Mao, referring to the old Shan State of [Mong Mao](/wiki/Mong_Mao "Mong Mao"). Tai Long is used to refer to the Southern Shan State dialect spoken in southern and central regions west of the [Salween River](/wiki/Salween_River "Salween River"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) the Northern Shan State dialect,[[7]](#cite_note-7) and the dialect spoken in [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"). There are also dialects still spoken by a small number of people in [Kachin State](/wiki/Kachin_State "Kachin State"), such as [Tai Laing](/wiki/Tai_Laing_language "Tai Laing language"), and [Khamti](/wiki/Khamti_language "Khamti language") spoken in northern [Sagaing Region](/wiki/Sagaing_Region "Sagaing Region").

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Phonology")]

### Consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Consonants")]

Shan has 19 consonants. Unlike [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") and [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language") ([Isan](/wiki/Isan_language "Isan language")) there are no voiced plosives /d/ and /b/.

|  | | [Labial](/wiki/Labial_consonant "Labial consonant") | [Dental](/wiki/Dental_consonant "Dental consonant")/ [Alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar_consonant "Alveolar consonant") | ([Alveolo-](/wiki/Alveolo-palatal_consonant "Alveolo-palatal consonant")) [Palatal](/wiki/Palatal_consonant "Palatal consonant") | [Velar](/wiki/Velar_consonant "Velar consonant") | [Glottal](/wiki/Glottal_consonant "Glottal consonant") |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Nasal](/wiki/Nasal_consonant "Nasal consonant") | | /[m](/wiki/Voiced_bilabial_nasal "Voiced bilabial nasal")/ မ | /[n](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_nasal "Voiced alveolar nasal")/ ၼ | /[ɲ](/wiki/Voiced_palatal_nasal "Voiced palatal nasal")/ ၺ | /[ŋ](/wiki/Voiced_velar_nasal "Voiced velar nasal")/ င |  |
| [Plosive](/wiki/Plosive "Plosive") | [unaspirated](/wiki/Unaspirated "Unaspirated") | /[p](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_plosive "Voiceless bilabial plosive")/ ပ | /[t](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_plosive "Voiceless alveolar plosive")/ တ | /[tɕ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolo-palatal_affricate "Voiceless alveolo-palatal affricate")/ ၸ | /[k](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_plosive "Voiceless velar plosive")/ ၵ | /[ʔ](/wiki/Glottal_stop "Glottal stop")/[[a]](#cite_note-8) ဢ |
| [aspirated](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant") | /[pʰ](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant")/ ၽ | /[tʰ](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant")/ ထ |  | /[kʰ](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant")/ ၶ |  |
| [Fricative](/wiki/Fricative "Fricative") | | (/[f](/wiki/Voiceless_labiodental_fricative "Voiceless labiodental fricative")/)[[b]](#cite_note-9) ၾ | /[s](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_fricative "Voiceless alveolar fricative")/ သ |  |  | /[h](/wiki/Voiceless_glottal_fricative "Voiceless glottal fricative")/ ႁ |
| [Trill](/wiki/Trill_consonant "Trill consonant") | |  | (/[r](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_trill "Voiced alveolar trill")/)[[c]](#cite_note-10) ရ |  |  |  |
| [Approximant](/wiki/Approximant "Approximant") | |  |  | /[j](/wiki/Voiced_palatal_approximant "Voiced palatal approximant")/ ယ | /[w](/wiki/Voiced_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Voiced labial–velar approximant")/ ဝ |  |
| [Lateral](/wiki/Lateral_consonant "Lateral consonant") | |  | /[l](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_lateral_approximant "Voiced alveolar lateral approximant")/ လ |  |  |  |

1. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** The glottal plosive is implied after a short vowel without final, or the silent 'a' before a vowel.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** Initial [f] is only found in eastern dialects in words that are pronounced with [pʰ] elsewhere.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** The trill is very rare and mainly used in Pali and some English loan words, sometimes as a glide in initial consonant clusters. Many Shans find it difficult to pronounce [r], often pronouncing it [l].

### Vowels and diphthongs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Vowels and diphthongs")]

Shan has ten vowels and 13 diphthongs:

|  | [Front](/wiki/Front_vowel "Front vowel") | [Central](/wiki/Central_vowel "Central vowel") | [Back](/wiki/Back_vowel "Back vowel") |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Close](/wiki/Close_vowel "Close vowel") | /[i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel")/ | /[ɨ](/wiki/Close_central_unrounded_vowel "Close central unrounded vowel")/~/[ɯ](/wiki/Close_back_unrounded_vowel "Close back unrounded vowel")/ | /[u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")/ |
| [Mid](/wiki/Mid_vowel "Mid vowel") | /[e](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel")/ | /[ə](/wiki/Mid_central_vowel "Mid central vowel")/~/[ɤ](/wiki/Close-mid_back_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid back unrounded vowel")/ | /[o](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel")/ |
| [Open](/wiki/Open_vowel "Open vowel") | /[ɛ](/wiki/Open-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Open-mid front unrounded vowel")/ | /[a](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel")/ /[aː](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel")/ | /[ɔ](/wiki/Open-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Open-mid back rounded vowel")/ |

[iw], [ew], [ɛw]; [uj], [oj], [ɯj], [ɔj], [ɤj]; [aj], [aɯ], [aw]; [aːj], [aːw]

Shan has less vowel complexity than Thai, and Shan people learning Thai have difficulties with sounds such as "ia," "ua," and "uea" [ɯa]. Triphthongs are absent. Shan has no systematic distinction between long and short vowels characteristic of Thai.

### Tones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Tones")]

Shan has [phonemic](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") contrasts among the [tones](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") of syllables. There are five to six tonemes in Shan, depending on the dialect. The sixth tone is only spoken in the north; in other parts it is only used for emphasis.

#### Contrastive tones in unchecked syllables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Contrastive tones in unchecked syllables")]

The table below presents six phonemic tones in unchecked syllables, i.e. closed syllables ending in [sonorant sounds](/wiki/Sonorant "Sonorant") such as [m], [n], [ŋ], [w], and [j] and open syllables.

| No. | Description | IPA | Description | Transcription\* | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | rising (24) | ˨˦ | Starting rather low and rising pitch | ǎ | a (not marked) |
| 2 | low (11) | ˩ | Low, even pitch | à | a, |
| 3 | mid-falling (32) | ˧˨ | Medium level pitch, slightly falling in the end | [a](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") (not marked) | a; |
| 4 | high (55) | ˥ | High, even pitch | á | a: |
| 5 | high-falling and creaky (42) | ˦˨ˀ | Short, creaky, strongly falling with lax final glottal stop | âʔ, â̰ | a. |
| 6 | emphatic (343) or middle (33) | ˧˦˧ / ˧ | Starting mid level, then slightly rising, with a drop at the end (similar to tones 3 and 5) | [a᷈](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") | a- |

:   \* The symbol in the first transcription column corresponds to conventions used for other tonal languages; the second is derived from the Shan orthography.

The following table shows an example of the [phonemic](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") tones:

| Tone | Shan | IPA | Transliteration | English |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| rising | ၼႃ | /nǎː/ | na | *thick* |
| low | ၼႃႇ | /nàː/ | na, | *very* |
| mid-falling | ၼႃႈ | /nà̱ː/ | na; | *face* |
| high | ၼႃး | /náː/ | na: | *paddy field* |
| high-falling and creaky | ၼႃႉ | /nâ̰(ː)/ | na. | *aunt, uncle* |
| emphatic or middle | ၼႃႊ | /nāː/ | na- | (for interjection / transcription) |

The Shan tones correspond to Thai tones as follows:

1. The Shan rising tone is close to the Thai rising tone.
2. The Shan low tone is equivalent to the Thai low tone.
3. The Shan mid-tone is different from the Thai mid-tone. It falls in the end.
4. The Shan high tone is close to the Thai high tone. But it is not rising.
5. The Shan falling tone is different from the Thai falling tone. It is short, creaky and ends with a glottal stop.

#### Contrastive tones in checked syllables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Contrastive tones in checked syllables")]

The table below presents four phonemic tones in checked syllables, i.e. closed syllables ending in a [glottal stop](/wiki/Glottal_stop "Glottal stop") [ʔ] and [obstruent sounds](/wiki/Obstruent "Obstruent") such as [p], [t], and [k].

| Tone | Shan | Phonemic | Phonetic | Transliteration | English |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| high | လၵ်း | /lák/ | [lak˥] | lak: | *post* |
| creaky | လၵ်ႉ | /la̰k/ | [la̰k˦˨ˀ] | lak. | *steal* |
| low | လၢၵ်ႇ | /làːk/ | [laːk˩] | laak, | *differ from others* |
| mid | လၢၵ်ႈ | /lāːk/ | [laːk˧˨] | laak; | *drag* |

### Syllable structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Syllable structure")]

The [syllable](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable") structure of Shan is C(G)V((V)/(C)), which is to say the [onset](/wiki/Syllable_onset "Syllable onset") consists of a consonant optionally followed by a [glide](/wiki/Semivowel "Semivowel"), and the [rhyme](/wiki/Syllable_rime "Syllable rime") consists of a monophthong alone, a monophthong with a consonant, or a diphthong alone. (Only in some dialects, a diphthong may also be followed by a consonant)

The glides are: -w-, -y- and -r-.
There are seven possible final consonants: /ŋ/, /n/, /m/, /k/, /t/, /p/, and /ʔ/.

Some representative words are:

* CV /kɔ/ *also*
* CVC /kàːt/ *market*
* CGV /kwàː/ *to go*
* CGVC /kwaːŋ/ *broad*
* CVV /kǎi/ *far*
* CGVV /kwáːi/ *water buffalo*

Typical Shan words are monosyllabic. Multisyllabic words are mostly Pali loanwords, or Burmese words with the initial weak syllable /ə/.

## Resources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Resources")]

Given the present instabilities in Burma, one choice for scholars is to study the Shan people and their language in Thailand, where estimates of Shan refugees run as high as two million, and [Mae Hong Son Province](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son_Province "Mae Hong Son Province") is home to a Shan majority. The major source for information about the Shan language in English is Dunwoody Press's *Shan for English Speakers*. They also publish a Shan-English dictionary. Aside from this, the language is almost completely undescribed in English.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
